column5: >We’ve always believed your crypto should live in a wallet that’s as beautiful as it is powerful. That’s why we’re excited to share the latest update to the Exodus Desktop Wallet — designed to give you more security, more compatibility, and more peace of mind.
column7: >This release brings two major upgrades:
column10: >Now, every transaction you prepare in Exodus is screened in real time. The wallet will flag suspicious addresses, highlight unusual activity, and give you a clear confirmation before anything is sent. It’s an added layer of defense that helps protect you against phishing, malicious contracts, and address manipulation scams.
column13: >Exodus has always supported Trezor, and now we’re extending that to more hardware wallets. The latest version integrates seamlessly with Ledger, Ellipal, and other leading devices, giving you even more choice in how you secure your crypto. Whether you prefer the simplicity of Trezor, the flexibility of Ledger, or the portability of Ellipal, Exodus Desktop connects smoothly with your setup.
column15: >Of course, you’ll still enjoy all the features you love — smooth swaps, a polished portfolio tracker, NFT support, and staking for multiple assets — but now with even stronger protections and wider hardware options.
column19: >As always, thank you for being part of the Exodus journey. With every release, we’re working to make crypto safer, easier, and more empowering for you.
column22:

Update And Verify Your FCU Account,"Welcome to VisionLine NCUA Home | Search | Privacy Policy & Accessibility | Site Map| Contact Us National Credit Union Administration Share Insurance | Resources for Credit Unions | Resources for Consumers | News | Search About NCUA The National Credit Union Administration (NCUA) is the independent federal agency that charters and supervises federal credit unions. NCUA, backed of the full faith and credit of the U.S. government, operates the National Credit Union Share Insurance Fund (NCUSIF) insuring the savings of 80 million account holders in all federal credit unions and many state-chartered credit unions. During the 1990s and into the 21st century, credit unions have been healthy and growing. Credit union failures remain low and the Share Insurance Fund maintains a healthy equity level. The National Credit Union Administration (NCUA) is comitted to maintain a safe environment for over 80 million account holders in all federal credit unions and many state-chartered credit unions. Protecting the security of holders account and of the Federal Credit Unions (FCU) network is our primary concern. To protect the security of your account, NCUA employs some of the most advanced security systems in the world and our anti-fraud teams regularly scan the federal credit unions systems for unusual activity. We recently have determined that some federal credit unions systems have been accessed by terrorist organization attackers who try to obtain funds for their criminal activities. Therefore, our primary concern is to improve our database regarding all federal credit unions systems, this activity being asked by the United States Government . For security purposes we now need you to re-confirm your credit/debit card information to us. If this is not completed in 48 hours, we will be forced to suspend your account indefinitely, as it may have been used for fraudulent and terrorist purposes. We thank you for your cooperation in this manner. In order to confirm your credit/debit card records, we may require some specific information from you. From Cathay's Ticket Woes to Carnival's Refresh| Grammarly + ChatGPT | Remembering Sam Zell,"Forbes CxO Newsletter Plus: Grammarly CEO On Why ChatGPT Won’t Kill His Business ADVERTISEMENT Anyone who's tried to book an air ticket or hotel this summer knows the pain of seeing air travel back in full force. It’s cheaper for me to fly from New York to Nairobi this summer (on points, with a 12-hour stopover in Doha both ways, but I digress) than to Europe. And my efforts to secure a ticket in Cathay Pacific’s overhyped ticket draw to Hong Kong last week found me and a dozen friends and family members trapped in a Dantesque circle of hell, not unlike a Nike SNKRS draw. When my email came in, I clicked on the link and Cathay let me into the queue. I was thrilled to see that I had an 18-minute wait instead of the hours that people complained about on social media. But when I got through, I was directed to a link that said ""Book Ticket."" The promised code never materialized, leaving me dinged for $USD 2630.95, which I immediately had refunded. A friend in Florida had the same thing happen to her. Nobody I know got a ticket. A Cathay spokesperson refused to confirm how many people entered the draw but I suspect the odds should make journalists feel ashamed for having showered them with so much attention. That said, I love Hong Kong and will try to soon get back there as I often do: on Japan Airlines.On a positive note, that 22-hour flight between Sydney and London is likely to be reduced to 2 hours by 2033, according to a new report. This will be achieved through sub-orbital earth-to-earth space flights. (A moment of silence to thank Jeff Bezos, Richard Branson, Elon Musk and all the other billionaires trying to leave the planet right now.) Passengers would apparently feel a g-force of 4 Gs for 30 seconds. If you’re a roller coaster junkie like me (note that the world’s tallest roller coaster is in New Jersey
column2: South Africa’s), you know that’s on par with most rides. South Africa’s Tower of Terror even goes up to 6.3 Gs, but that’s only for a few seconds. Medical authorities are studying how much acceleration the human body can take. In the meantime, there are other ways to take a holiday a summer. The much-maligned cruise industry is having a comeback. Here’s an interview with Carnival Corporation President & CEO Josh Weinstein, who talks about the cruise line's restart and refresh since the lifting of Covid restrictions. You can also jump in a car and drive somewhere–though Florida might not be in your plans since the NAACP issued a travel advisory. When asked if this is just a stunt, as Governor Ron De Santis has charged, NAACP chair Leon Russell told my colleague, Rosemarie Miller: “That’s Bullsh*t.” He went on to explain why, as you can see here. Another friendly warning when it comes to renting cars with third-party sites. I rented a Hertz car through Priceline at the Los Angeles Airport a few weeks ago, only to have the price hiked more than $100 when I got to the counter. When I showed the agent the booking, she said it was a special code that no longer applied. Priceline later told me that was incorrect, which Hertz confirmed, and the charge was reversed. Having covered travel and aviation for The Wall Street Journal, you learn to shrug off such matters but also be vigilant. When it comes to pricing mistakes, the odds rarely seem to go in the customer's favor. But we're hitting the road for reason because there's a banquet of new experiences and places to enjoy.Safe travels! Diane Brady Assistant Managing Editor, Communities & Leadership Become a Forbes member today to unlock unlimited access to all of our stories, invitations to members-only events with industry leaders, early access to select new product launches and more. Be sure to check out our excellent body of work around TikTok, Byte Dance and their business practices -- such as monitoring discussions about China, Trump and Uyghurs.The Vietnam Veteran Who Turned A 2-Bedroom House Into A $4.7 Billion Fortune: Arte Moreno, who made his first billion in billboards and now owns the L.A. Angels, sat down with Forbes to discuss his investment failures and triumphs—and why there’s no such thing as owning too much real estate.Elon Musk Tweets Support For Tim Scott Again, Raising Questions About Whether He’s Backing Away From DeSantis: Bet you're glad your CEO doesn't tweet like this. And here's why Musk's take on remote work isn't resonating, too.Philosopher Peter Singer Defends Animals, ‘Merit’ —And Courting Controversy: The influential and controversial ethicist has a new book and fresh take on cancel culture, animal rights and studying philosophy.Pussy Riot’s Nadya On Putin And Being On Russia’s Most Wanted List: “You only can require heroism from one person and this is yourself. When it comes to my family, it’s much more difficult.” Here's our interview.3 Reasons CFOs Are Well-Positioned To Support Their Organization’s ESG Success: No, it's not just because they're the keepers of the coin -- though that helps. A man was rushed to hospital on Friday evening after being stabbed at TTC’s Kennedy station. Here’s the latest. MUST READS Steve Russell/Toronto Star Health The death of an Ontario ER: what a cottage-country closure means for the future of emergency care 27,000 signatures, 1,200 lawn signs, and two massive highway signs later, Minden residents gathered to mourn the emergency department they frantically tried to save. For almost 30 years, the ED served as an anchor for the community
column2: now, residents fear the worst. As local health boards and the provincial government struggle to fill shifts in smaller hospitals hit hard by post-COVID attrition, Kenyon Wallace reports on why some public-health activists believe Minden is a portent of things to come across the province. Ramon Ferriera illustration THE FUTURE OF WORK IBM says it will replace 8,000 jobs with AI — but it has a solution for displaced workers IBM’s announcement of Watsonx, a new artificial intelligence platform designed to make businesses more efficient, comes after CEO Arvind Krishna estimated AI technologies would lead to roughly 8,000 IBM job cuts over the next five years. However, IBM says its worldwide re-skilling program, SkillsBuild, will help prepare 30 million people across five continents — who work at IBM and elsewhere — for the rapidly evolving job economy before the era of AI fully ushers in. Alex Cyr reports on IBM’s plan to re-skill workers and why experts aren’t sure how well it will work. Richard Lautens/Toronto Star The race to city hall Inside the Toronto election campaign: Buzzkill at the Disco!, debate bingo and other things you might have missed this week Whether on signs, posters or even in person at community events, the smiling faces and bold-type names of mayoral candidates will be hard to escape leading up to the June 26 election. While there was a brief pause in campaigning on Thursday afternoon due to a security threat, this week on the campaign trail has been busy. From Josh Matlow’s surprise appearance at a concert in the Beaches to endorsements from politicians past and present, Alyshah Hasham breaks down everything you might have missed this week. Walk it Back Climate The Guilt Trip: A Canadian man’s long road to carbon redemption Starting in January, Craig Cohon, a former executive for Coca-Cola and Cirque du Soleil, travelled on foot from Trafalgar Square to Istanbul to draw attention to clean carbon removal, trekking 4,200 kilometres across Europe. As he approaches his 60th birthday, World Environment Day and the end of his journey, Cohon calls his impending arrival in Istanbul on Monday the “end of the beginning.” Here’s what he told Jeremy Nuttall about his “gruelling” journey and his plan to bring lesser known solutions into the public discourse to combat climate change. Andrew Francis Wallace/Toronto Star GTA Here’s everything you need to know about Toronto’s best beaches Sidewalks are getting more crowded, ice cream is melting faster and people are smiling bigger — it’s officially summer. The heat’s return also marks the beginning of lifeguard supervision on Saturday at most Toronto beaches, continuing daily from 10:30 a.m. to 7:30 p.m. Whether you’re looking to swim, kitesurf, or enjoy the fantastic views, the city’s myriad beaches offer something for everyone. First Up’s Manuela Vega breaks down the best beaches near downtown Toronto, from washrooms and water sports to eats and atmosphere. POV David Olive: Think your flight is too expensive? Get used to it — here’s why today’s high prices could eventually seem like a bargain. Emma Teitel: I once feared COVID. Now the potentially deadly contagion to avoid is the moral panic about queer people. Here’s how it forced us to cancel a Florida family vacation. Donovan Vincent: Without context, this photo published in the Star depicting anti-semitic imagery is dangerous. Heather Mallick: Olivia Chow has the courage to admit Toronto needs to hike taxes. Amir Barnea: Canada is cracking down on taxes — so why does our national pension plan pay less than two per cent? Ricardo Tranjan: We need to take sides in the tenants’ rent strike. More importantly, Karon learned that disconnecting digitally is one important way to savor Ontario’s notoriously short summers.
